Were You Asked This?
Design a large model file distribution system

Design a system that efficiently distributes 500GB machine learning model files from an external repository to 1000 datacenter servers over a 10Gbps link, optimizing for minimal transfer time while ensuring each server receives a complete copy.

Asked at:

Anthropic

NVIDIA

Google

A model file distribution service is an internal content delivery system that moves very large machine learning model files (hundreds of gigabytes) from an external repository into a datacenter and rapidly replicates them to thousands of servers. Think of it like a BitTorrent-style, datacenter-local CDN for artifacts, similar in spirit to how Apple or Netflix stage content and then fan it out efficiently.

Interviewers ask this to see if you can reason from first principles about bandwidth bottlenecks, peer-assisted fanout, chunking, integrity verification, and scheduling under constraints. They‚Äôre testing whether you can separate control plane from data plane, design for failure and observability, and turn a single 10Gbps ingress into a fast, reliable, many-to-many transfer without melting the network or corrupting data.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to request distribution of a specific model version to a target set of servers and datacenter(s).

Users should be able to monitor real-time progress and verify that each target server has a complete, checksum-verified copy of the model.

Users should be able to resume interrupted transfers automatically and recover from partial or corrupted downloads without restarting from scratch.

Users should be able to control rollout parameters (e.g., priority, bandwidth caps, scheduling windows) to minimize impact on production traffic.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How would you minimize end-to-end completion time given a single 10Gbps external link and 1000 targets?

How will you ensure data integrity, resumability, and correctness for 500GB artifacts?

How do you control contention to avoid saturating NICs and fabric (incast) while still maximizing throughput?

What does your control plane look like for orchestration, failure recovery, and safe rollouts/rollbacks?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Handling Large Blobs

The artifact is a 500GB file, so chunking, manifests, content hashing, resumable transfers, and atomic activation are essential to ensure integrity and to avoid restarting massive downloads after transient failures.

Read More
Scaling Reads

You must turn a single copy from an external source into 1000 local copies quickly; peer-assisted fanout, tree replication, and swarm-style distribution are classic ways to scale reads without overloading the origin.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

There is an explicit 10Gbps ingress bottleneck and per-host NIC limits; you need rate limiting, scheduling, rack-awareness, and backpressure to prevent incast and keep the network stable while maximizing throughput.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis is a strong fit for fast, ephemeral control-plane state: per-host heartbeats, piece availability maps, rate-limit tokens, and lightweight queues. Its in-memory speed helps the tracker react quickly to churn and rebalance peers.

Read More
PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL provides durable storage for distribution jobs, model manifests/metadata, target sets, audit logs, and rollout policies. It‚Äôs reliable, well-known in interviews, and a solid backbone for the control plane.

Read More
ZooKeeper

ZooKeeper (or a similar coordinator) can handle leader election for the orchestrator, maintain small-scale service discovery, and provide consistent membership/state watches so the control plane remains highly available.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Dropbox

Both systems manage large file synchronization with chunking, hashing, resumable transfers, and eventual consistency across many clients. The main difference is that this problem emphasizes intra-datacenter fanout and speed.

Read
Practice
Job Scheduler

Distributing to 1000 servers is an orchestration problem: you assign work, track progress, handle retries, and stage rollouts. The control-plane concerns (heartbeats, retries, dependencies) mirror a distributed scheduler‚Äôs design.

Read
Practice
Distributed Cache

Like populating a cache cluster, you‚Äôre moving data to many nodes and must manage replication topology, membership, and failure handling. Gossip/peer-to-peer concepts and rack-aware placement are directly relevant.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Fetching directly from the external repository to all 1000 servers

This ignores the 10Gbps ingress bottleneck and would take unacceptably long, hammering the origin. Without staging and peer-assisted fanout, total completion time explodes and the external link stays saturated for hours or days.

No chunking or end-to-end integrity verification

Treating the 500GB artifact as a single blob means any failure restarts the entire transfer and silent corruption can slip through. Missing per-chunk hashes and whole-file checksums leads to unreliable deployments.

Lack of congestion control and topology awareness

Starting all downloads at once or allowing unlimited peers per host causes incast, NIC oversubscription, and degraded production traffic. Without rate limits, rack-aware scheduling, and backpressure, the network becomes unstable.
